mnemonics.title: Normal Form Progression
mnemonics.content:
1NF: Atoms Only (no lists/arrays)
2NF: No Partial Keys (full dependency)
3NF: No Transitive Keys (direct only)
4NF: No Multi-Values (independent data)
BCNF: Keys Everywhere (determinants are superkeys)

mnemonics.title: Anomaly Types
mnemonics.content:
Insert: Can't add new data (missing info)
Delete: Lose related data (unintended)
Update: Same data, many places (inconsistency)

mnemonics.title: Normalization Steps
mnemonics.content: CANDID: Check Atomic values, Analyze Keys, Normalize Decompose, Eliminate partials, Test dependencies, Integrity, Dependency preservation

acronyms.usage: Used to express relationships between attributes in normalization theory

acronyms.expansion: Candidate Key - minimal set of attributes that uniquely identify tuples
acronyms.usage: Used to identify prime attributes and determine normal forms

acronyms.expansion: Boyce-Codd Normal Form - stricter version of 3NF
acronyms.usage: Applied when 3NF doesn't adequately handle certain dependency patterns

acronyms.expansion: First through Fifth Normal Forms - progressive normalization levels
acronyms.usage: Used to measure how well-structured a database relation is

visualTips.visualization: Think of FD like a function: input X gives unique output Y. Draw an arrow from X to Y showing the direction of dependency. X is the 'determinant' (left side), Y is the 'dependent' (right side).
Draw three circles in a chain: A→B→C. The dependency A→C is transitive because it goes through B. Cut the middle link to achieve 3NF.
In a composite key (A,B)→C, draw two boxes A and B feeding into C. If only A reaches C (without B), that's a partial dependency - draw it as a 'broken' connection.
Draw a Venn diagram: BCNF circle inside 3NF circle inside 2NF circle. Every relation in BCNF is in 3NF, but not vice versa.

analogies.analogy: Like sugar cubes in a bowl - each piece is indivisible. You cannot have a 'bucket of sugar' as one item; it must be measured in individual cubes or grams.
Like a bicycle where both wheels must be attached to the frame for it to work properly. Taking only one wheel (partial) makes the bike non-functional.
Like a direct flight vs. connecting flight. Direct (A→C) is better than transitive (A→B→C) because if the middle stop (B) changes, the final destination is affected.
Like organizing a library: books (data) should be categorized by subject (tables) with each book having a unique ISBN (primary key). Having horror and romance in the same book causes confusion.
Like keeping a printed phone directory (denormalized) vs. searching online (normalized). Sometimes having redundant copies is faster for read-heavy operations.
